title: C# Advanced — Paper 5
description: Advanced C# covering Lambda & Functional Patterns, Delegates, Events, Async/Await, Task Parallel Library, Attributes, Extension Methods, Expression Trees, Dynamic Types, and Advanced Pattern Matching.

questions.text: What is the difference between Task.Run() and a plain async method call?

options.text: Task.Run() and async method calls are identical in behavior.
Task.Run() offloads synchronous or CPU-bound work to the thread pool; an async method call begins execution synchronously on the current thread until it hits an await, then returns a Task.
Task.Run() creates a dedicated new thread each time.
Async methods cannot be called without Task.Run().

questions.text: What does Parallel.ForEach do differently from a regular foreach loop?

options.text: Parallel.ForEach executes iterations sequentially with extra overhead.
Parallel.ForEach partitions the data source and executes iterations concurrently across multiple thread pool threads, exploiting multi-core processors for CPU-bound work.
Parallel.ForEach requires a CancellationToken to run.
Parallel.ForEach guarantees iteration order is preserved.

questions.text: What is advanced pattern matching with a positional pattern in C# 8+?

options.text: A pattern that matches elements by position in an array.
A pattern that deconstructs an object using its Deconstruct method or tuple structure, matching parts of the object by their positional components in a switch expression or is expression.
A pattern that uses index-based access like list[0] in a switch.
A positional pattern uses string positions to match substrings.

questions.text: What is a monad-like pattern in C# and how does it relate to Func<T,TResult> chaining?

options.text: Monads are only available in functional languages, not C#.

A monad-like pattern wraps a value in a context (like Maybe<T> or Task<T>) and provides a Bind/Map operation (similar to SelectMany) that chains transformations while handling the context (null, async, error) automatically.
A monad is a design pattern that uses multiple inheritance.
Monadic patterns require the dynamic keyword in C#.

questions.text: How does removing a delegate that was never added behave in C#?

options.text: It throws an InvalidOperationException.
Delegate.Remove (and the -= operator) silently does nothing if the specified delegate is not found in the invocation list; no exception is thrown.
It removes the last delegate in the invocation list instead.
It clears all delegates from the invocation list.

questions.text: What is the difference between IObservable<T> and events in C# for reactive programming?

options.text: IObservable<T> and events are identical; Rx is just syntactic sugar.

IObservable<T> is a composable, filterable, and transformable stream with built-in error and completion signals; events are simpler and lack composition operators, error channels, and lifetime management.
Events support LINQ operators natively; IObservable<T> does not.
IObservable<T> is a built-in C# language feature like events.

questions.text: What is PLINQ and how is it enabled on an IEnumerable<T> query?

options.text: PLINQ is a parallel version of LINQ available only via NuGet.
PLINQ (Parallel LINQ) executes LINQ queries in parallel across multiple threads; it is enabled by calling .AsParallel() on the source, converting it to a ParallelQuery<T>.
PLINQ is enabled by replacing all Select calls with ParallelSelect.
PLINQ requires Task.Run() to wrap each LINQ operator.

questions.text: What does the 'not' pattern do in C# 9+ pattern matching?

options.text: It matches null values only.
It negates another pattern: 'x is not null' matches when x is not null; 'x is not string' matches when x is not a string.
It is equivalent to the ! operator applied to a boolean expression.
It matches the logical NOT of a switch arm.

questions.text: How does the [Flags] attribute on an enum change its behavior?

options.text: It forces the enum to use byte storage.

It indicates that the enum values are bit flags that can be combined with bitwise operators; it also makes Enum.ToString() display combined flag names (e.g., 'Read, Write') instead of a numeric value.
It prevents duplicate enum values.
It enables LINQ queries over enum values.

questions.text: What does the INotifyPropertyChanged interface and pattern require?

options.text: It requires implementing a single static method called NotifyAll().
It requires implementing the PropertyChanged event of type PropertyChangedEventHandler; when a property changes, raise the event passing the property name so bound UI elements can update.
It automatically propagates property changes without any code.
It requires marking each property with the [Notify] attribute.

questions.text: How does dynamic dispatch in C# differ from virtual method dispatch?

options.text: Dynamic dispatch uses vtable lookup; virtual dispatch uses the DLR.

Virtual method dispatch is resolved at compile time via vtable and operates on static types; dynamic dispatch defers member lookup entirely to runtime via the DLR, operating on values typed as 'dynamic'.
They are identical; dynamic is just a shorthand for virtual.
Virtual dispatch is always slower than dynamic dispatch.

questions.text: What does the 'and' pattern combinator do in C# 9+ switch expressions?

options.text: It matches if either of two patterns matches.
It matches only when both combined patterns match simultaneously: e.g., 'x is > 0 and < 100' matches when x is both greater than 0 and less than 100.
It is used to combine multiple switch arms.
It concatenates two string patterns.

questions.text: How do you build an expression tree that represents a member access (reading a property)?

options.text: Expression.Property is not available in the System.Linq.Expressions API.
var param = Expression.Parameter(typeof(Person), "p"); var prop = Expression.Property(param, "Name"); var lambda = Expression.Lambda<Func<Person,string>>(prop, param); var fn = lambda.Compile();
Expression.Member(typeof(Person), "Name").Compile()
Expression.Access(p => p.Name).Build()

questions.text: What is the Thread Local Storage (ThreadLocal<T>) pattern and how does it relate to parallel code?

options.text: ThreadLocal<T> shares one value across all threads.
ThreadLocal<T> gives each thread its own independent copy of a value, preventing data races in parallel code where each thread needs per-thread state (e.g., per-thread random number generator).
ThreadLocal<T> is equivalent to a lock for thread synchronization.
ThreadLocal<T> stores values in the thread pool's shared queue.

questions.text: What is the purpose of an extension method on IEnumerable<T> that returns IEnumerable<T> instead of void?

options.text: It allows the method to modify the original collection in place.
Returning IEnumerable<T> enables fluent LINQ-style chaining, where each extension method transforms and passes the sequence to the next operator without materializing intermediate results.
It prevents the method from being callable on concrete collections like List<T>.
It signals that the method uses lazy evaluation only if returning IQueryable<T>.

questions.text: What is the difference between Parallel.For and PLINQ for parallelism?

options.text: Parallel.For is for LINQ queries; PLINQ is for simple loops.
Parallel.For is imperative and used for index-based loops; PLINQ is declarative, extending LINQ with parallelism, better suited for data transformation pipelines.
PLINQ uses dedicated threads; Parallel.For uses the thread pool.
They are identical in behavior and performance.

questions.text: How do you implement a custom attribute that can be read at runtime to validate a property value?

options.text: Attributes are compile-time only and cannot be read at runtime.
Define a class inheriting from Attribute with validation logic; at runtime, use reflection to get the attribute via GetCustomAttribute<T>() and call the validation method, passing the property value.
Use [Validate] from the built-in System namespace.
Override the Validate() method in the Attribute base class.

questions.text: What is the difference between a property pattern and a type pattern in C# pattern matching?

options.text: They are identical; type patterns check properties.

A type pattern (x is TypeName t) checks the runtime type and optionally binds; a property pattern ({ PropertyName: value }) tests specific property values of an object (can be combined with a type pattern).
Property patterns only work with record types.
Type patterns check at compile time; property patterns check at runtime.

questions.text: What happens when you call async void instead of async Task and an exception is thrown?

options.text: The exception is captured in the returned Task and can be awaited.
An exception in an async void method cannot be caught by the caller via try/catch around the call site; it propagates to the synchronization context and typically crashes the application.
Async void swallows all exceptions silently.
The compiler prevents throwing exceptions in async void methods.

questions.text: How does an Expression<Func<T, bool>> differ from Func<T, bool> when used for filtering?

options.text: Func<T,bool> can be translated to SQL; Expression<Func<T,bool>> cannot.

Func<T,bool> is a compiled delegate executed in-memory by LINQ to Objects; Expression<Func<T,bool>> is an expression tree that IQueryable providers (like EF) translate to SQL or other query languages.
They behave identically on both IEnumerable<T> and IQueryable<T>.
Expression<Func<T,bool>> executes faster because it bypasses the delegate call overhead.

questions.text: What does the 'or' pattern combinator match in C# 9+?

options.text: It matches only if both patterns match.
It matches if at least one of the combined patterns matches: e.g., 'x is 1 or 2 or 3' matches when x equals 1, 2, or 3.
It is equivalent to || on boolean expressions.
It matches the union of types in a type hierarchy.

questions.text: What is the maximum number of type parameters that Func<> supports in the .NET standard library?

options.text: 8 type parameters
16 type parameters (Func<T1,...,T16,TResult>)
4 type parameters
32 type parameters

questions.text: What is the purpose of ParallelOptions.MaxDegreeOfParallelism in Parallel.For/ForEach?

options.text: It sets the priority of the parallel tasks.
It caps the maximum number of concurrent threads/tasks used by the parallel operation, preventing over-subscription and resource exhaustion.
It controls the batch size of elements per thread.
It sets the thread affinity for parallel operations.

questions.text: How does dynamic interop with COM objects work in C#?

options.text: COM objects must be wrapped in a managed proxy class before use.

Using the dynamic keyword, C# can call COM methods and access COM properties directly without explicitly casting or using reflection; the DLR handles the dispatch, removing the need for verbose interop code.
COM interop in C# requires the unsafe keyword.
Dynamic COM calls always require calling Marshal.ReleaseComObject().

questions.text: When should you prefer async/await over manual continuation-passing with Task.ContinueWith()?

options.text: ContinueWith is always preferred because it offers more control.

async/await is preferred in most cases because it generates readable, linear-looking code, handles exceptions naturally, captures context correctly, and avoids the complexity of chained ContinueWith callbacks.
They are identical in all scenarios.
async/await only works with I/O-bound tasks; ContinueWith works for all tasks.

questions.text: What is the list pattern in C# 11+ pattern matching?

options.text: A pattern that matches any IEnumerable<T> with a LINQ expression.
A pattern that matches sequences by their elements: e.g., [1, 2, ..] matches a list starting with 1 and 2, and [..] matches any sequence.
A pattern that matches only List<T> types.
A pattern that enumerates all elements and checks their types.

questions.text: What does the [NonSerialized] attribute do when applied to a field?

options.text: It encrypts the field during serialization.
It excludes the field from binary serialization, so it is skipped by BinaryFormatter; the field will have its default value after deserialization.
It marks the field as write-only during deserialization.
It prevents the field from being accessed by reflection.

questions.text: What is the difference between an instance extension method and a static helper method?

options.text: They compile to identical IL; the only difference is calling syntax.

An extension method is called with instance syntax (obj.Method()) and participates in IntelliSense autocomplete on the type; a static helper requires explicit class qualification (Helper.Method(obj)) and does not appear in instance member lists.
Extension methods can access private members; static helpers cannot.
Static helpers can be inherited; extension methods cannot.

questions.text: What is the Visitor pattern and how do expression trees embody it?

options.text: Visitor pattern and expression trees are unrelated.

The Visitor pattern separates operations on a structure from the structure itself; expression trees embody this via ExpressionVisitor, which lets you walk and transform nodes of an expression tree without modifying the tree classes.
Expression trees use the Iterator pattern, not Visitor.
The Visitor pattern is automatically applied when compiling expression trees.

questions.text: What is the purpose of Parallel.For's localInit and localFinally parameters?

options.text: localInit runs before the loop; localFinally runs after all iterations globally.

localInit initializes per-thread local state before that thread's first iteration; localFinally aggregates the per-thread result into a shared value after that thread completes its work, enabling thread-safe reduction without locks in the inner loop.
They configure the thread pool before and after the loop.
localFinally is called for each iteration, not per thread.
